title: CHAPTER 3 —
PROMPTING AS SPECIFICATION
segments.body_html:
You send Claude a message: "Can you improve this introduction?"

Chapter 3 — Prompting as Specification — Two minutes later you have a rewritten paragraph. It is polished. The sentences are shorter. The word choice is cleaner.

beats.text: You send Claude a message: "Can you improve this introduction?" Two minutes later you have a rewritten paragraph. It is polished. The sentences are shorter. The word choice is cleaner. It is also completely wrong for your audience, your journal, and the argument you are making , because Claude had no idea what "better" meant to you.

segments.label: 01 / 12 · HOOK

segments.section: THE CORE CONCEPT: A PROMPT IS
title: THE CORE CONCEPT:
A PROMPT IS A WORK ORDER
segments.body_html: What to do→What to use→What to avoid→What the finished work should look like

beats.text: The research community has spent years studying prompting as a family of techniques for adapting language models to tasks through input design (Liu, Yuan, Fu, Jiang, Hayashi, and Neubig 2023). The practitioner version of that insight is simpler: a prompt is a specification of work. A good specification tells the contractor , human or AI , four things:

segments.label: 03 / 12 · THE CORE CONCEPT: A PROMPT IS

segments.section: THE SIX COMPONENTS OF A WELL-S
title: THE SIX COMPONENTS OF
A WELL-SPECIFIED PROMPT.
segments.body_html:
Task — What should Claude do?
Context — What does Claude need to know?
Source material — What evidence should Claude use?
Constraints — What must Claude avoid?

beats.text: Every effective prompt answers six questions. Not every prompt needs to answer all six in depth , a simple task may need only three. But knowing the six helps you diagnose why a prompt failed. The most commonly omitted components are source material, constraints, and evaluation criteria. When these are missing, Claude fills the gap with its best guess , which may be reasonable for a general reader and wrong for your specific situation.

segments.label: 04 / 12 · THE SIX COMPONENTS OF A WELL-S

title: WORKED WORKFLOW:
REVISING A RESEARCH METHODS SECTION
segments.body_html:
A bullet-point diagnosis (issue, severityhigh/medium/low, reason).
·A prioritized revision plan (what to fix first and why).
·An optional revised opening paragraph only if the opening is unclear.

beats.text: Here is how the same task looks before and after specification. "Can you improve the methods section of my paper?"

segments.section: WHY XML? WHY NOT JUST WRITE IT
title: WHY XML? WHY NOT JUST
WRITE IT OUT?.
segments.body_html:
You do not have to use XML tags. For a simple prompt with one or two components, plain prose or Markdown headings work f

Why XML? Why Not Just Write It Out? — XML tags become useful when your prompt has multiple sections and the model might confuse them (Anthropic, "Use XML tags

beats.text: You do not have to use XML tags. For a simple prompt with one or two components, plain prose or Markdown headings work fine. XML tags become useful when your prompt has multiple sections and the model might confuse them (Anthropic, "Use XML tags to structure your prompts"). A long source document pasted after a long instruction, for example, can blur the line between the source and the task. XML tags make the boundary visible to Claude and to you.

segments.label: 06 / 12 · WHY XML? WHY NOT JUST WRITE IT

title: THE REQUIREMENT-GATHERING
CONVERSATION.
segments.body_html:
Not every task starts with a complete specification. Sometimes you do not know exactly what you need until you start. In

The Requirement-Gathering Conversation — "I need help evaluating a research methods section, but I am not sure what angle is most useful. Ask me three questions

beats.text: Not every task starts with a complete specification. Sometimes you do not know exactly what you need until you start. In that case, the right first prompt is not a work order , it is a requirements conversation. "I need help evaluating a research methods section, but I am not sure what angle is most useful. Ask me three questions that will help you understand what I actually need, then propose a plan."

segments.label: 07 / 12 · THE REQUIREMENT-GATHERING CONV

segments.section: THE HUMAN GATE: WHAT TO VERIFY
title: THE HUMAN GATE:
WHAT TO VERIFY
segments.body_html:
The purposewhy this work matters and what it will be used for
The source constraintswhat evidence is valid, what is not
The evaluation criteriawhat "good" means in your institutional, scientific, or professional context
The final decisionwhether the output is acceptable for use
·Knowledge of your organization's unstated norms

beats.text: The specification frame clarifies what you still own. You must supply: The purpose , why this work matters and what it will be used for The source constraints , what evidence is valid, what is not The evaluation criteria , what "good" means in your institutional, scientific, or professional context The final decision , whether the output is acceptable for use

segments.label: 08 / 12 · THE HUMAN GATE: WHAT TO VERIFY

title: COMMON MISTAKES
segments.body_html: Asking for a finished artifact before asking for a diagnosis.→Using role prompts as a substitute for a task specification.→Treating XML or Markdown tags as magic.→Asking Claude to "make it better" without defining better.→Accepting a fluent output as a correct one.

beats.text: Asking for a finished artifact before asking for a diagnosis. Request diagnosis and plan before the rewrite. It is easier to correct a plan than a completed draft. Using role prompts as a substitute for a task specification. "Act as an expert researcher" tells Claude to adopt a tone, not to do specific work. Role prompts can supplement a specification but should not replace it. Specify the task first; add role context if the register or perspective matters.

segments.label: 09 / 12 · COMMON MISTAKES

title: BRIDGE TO
CHAPTER 4.
segments.body_html: Specification is the discipline that makes every other Claude workflow inspectable. The next chapter takes that discipli

beats.text: Specification is the discipline that makes every other Claude workflow inspectable. The next chapter takes that discipline into a different environment: a codebase. Claude Code is not a chat assistant that writes code. It is an agent that operates on your files, runs commands, and requires a different kind of supervision. The specification habits from this chapter carry forward , but the verification tools change.

segments.label: 10 / 12 · BRIDGE TO CHAPTER 4
